coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for the highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury ion in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Chaojie
Zhang, Duobao
Yang, Ran
Hu, Jingyu
Qu, Lingbo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S/GQDs) were synthesized by a simple and green pyrolysis method with citric acid as carbon source, d‑penicillamine as doped molecule. The obtained N,S/GQDs shows good water solubility, low cytotoxicity and high quantum yield of 56.4%. The doping of N and S can efficient promoted the coordination between the residual group of N,S/GQDs and Hg2+, thus, the fluorescence of N,S/GQDs can be dramatically and selectively quenched by Hg2+. Under the optimum conditions, the quenching effect of Hg2+ to N,S/GQDs was lined with its concentrations in the range of 0.9 to 30 nM with a detection limit of 0.69 nM. 100 fold of common metals had no influence for the determination of Hg2+. When Hela cells were incubated into the N,S/GQDs solutions for 2 h, more than 85% Cells still kept living even at a high concentration of 400 μg/mL suggesting the good biocompatibility and low toxicity of the obtained N,S/GQDs. Besides this, the N,S/GQDs also showed good cell permeability and could enter into the cell and showed bright blue light. Thus, they were successfully applied as a powerful fluorescent nano-sensor for imaging detection of mercury ions in the living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
d‑Penicillamine
Mercury ions
Living cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Establishment of the Detection System of N,S/GQDs-Hg2+
Selectivity of N,S/GQDs for Hg2+ Detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U140424 ), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Province ( 17A150051 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and size distribution of N,S/GQDs. (B) High-resolution TEM image of N,S/GQDs. (C) AFM image as well as their corresponding height profile (D). (E) XRD of N,S/GQDs.
(A) XPS spectra of the N,S/GQDs and high-resolution C1s (B), N1s (C), S2p (D) of the N,S/GQDs. (E) FT-IR of the N,S/GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N,S/GQDs and added 10 μM and 50 μM Hg2+. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S/GQDs and added 10 μM and 50 μM Hg2+. The fluorescent spectra of GQDs (C) and N,S/GQDs (D) before and after added 200 μM Hg2+ with 15 reaction minutes. (E) FT-IR of N,S/GQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the N,S/GQDs by addition Hg2+ with concentration ranging from 0 to 200 nM under excitation at 360 nm. (B) Plots of the values of (F0 − F)/F0 versus the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–200 nM. Insert: calibration plots of Hg2+ concentration versus (F0 − F)/F0 (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N,S/GQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively.

(A) MTT assay against HeLa cells upon 24 h of incubation different concentrations of N,S/GQDs. (B) Fluorescence images of control HeLa cells at 37 °C for 24 h. (C) HeLa cells incubated with the 50 μg/mL N,S/GQDs for 2 h at 37 °C and (D) further incubated with 40 μM Hg2+ for another 0.5 h at 37 °C.
Comparison of different fluorescence methods for Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ in water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots for the highly sensitive and selective detection of mercury ion in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S/GQDs) were synthesized by a simple and green pyrolysis method with citric acid as carbon source, d‑penicillamine as doped molecule. The obtained N,S/GQDs shows good water solubility, low cytotoxicity and high quantum yield of 56.4%. The doping of N and S can efficient promoted the coordination between the residual group of N,S/GQDs and Hg2+, thus, the fluorescence of N,S/GQDs can be dramatically and selectively quenched by Hg2+. Under the optimum conditions, the quenching effect of Hg2+ to N,S/GQDs was lined with its concentrations in the range of 0.9 to 30 nM with a detection limit of 0.69 nM. 100 fold of common metals had no influence for the determination of Hg2+. When Hela cells were incubated into the N,S/GQDs solutions for 2 h, more than 85% Cells still kept living even at a high concentration of 400 μg/mL suggesting the good biocompatibility and low toxicity of the obtained N,S/GQDs. Besides this, the N,S/GQDs also showed good cell permeability and could enter into the cell and showed bright blue light. Thus, they were successfully applied as a powerful fluorescent nano-sensor for imaging detection of mercury ions in the living cells.

A novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N,S/GQDs) were synthesized by a simple and green pyrolysis method with citric acid as carbon source, d‑penicillamine as doped molecule. The fluorescence of the obtained N,S/GQDs could be dramatically and selectively quenched by Hg2+ due to the coordination between them. Based on such a recognition effect, a very sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the imaging mercury ions in the living cells was developed.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (II) ion (Hg2+), one of the most toxic heavy metal pollutants, has been list as a priority pollutant by the World Health Organization and many Environmental Protection Agencies [1]. Even little intake of Hg2+ can also create a serious health risk to human body [2]. Hg2+ cannot be biodegradable, but can enrich through food chain and finally into the human body because of the strong interaction with the proteins and enzymes in the human body and ultimately make them inactivate [3]. In this case, developing an effective method for sensitive detection of Hg2+ in biological samples is very important for monitoring it and understanding its toxic mechanism. Up to now, many methods including atomic absorption [4], ICP-MS [5], luminescent [6], chemical [7], electrochemical [8] analytical methods have been developed for detecting mercury. Relative to the above methods, the fluorescence sensor approaches have received more and more favor due to their excellent sensitivity, fast response, ease of operation and ability to detect in a non-destructive manner [9]. Semiconductor quantum dots [10], organic chromophores [11], fluorescent conjugated polymers [12] have been used as the fluorescence probes for the detection of mercury ions. However, most of these publications were emphasized on the determination of mercury in the water. Furthermore, the employed probes are susceptible to oxidation, toxic and often have low photostability. Relative to the water environment, the detecting difficulties in biological environment will be greatly increased, which makes the detection have to meet higher requirements such as the higher sensitivity, selectivity and higher biocompatibility and stability, thus, synthesis of highly excellent fluorescence probes for imaging detection of mercury ions in the living cells is expectant.

As a new type of carbon nanomaterials, GQDs not only have adjustable optical properties, but also own good optical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity and other advantages [13,14], these unique physical and chemical properties make them have a good application prospects in optical sensors [15] and biological imaging [16]. However, because of the influences of quantum size effect and surface state, the pristine GQDs have low photoluminescence quantum yields [17] which limit their further applications. Recent years, it has been proved that the quantum yields of GQDs can be improved by using heteroatom doping, such as nitrogen [18], boron [19], sulfur [20], phosphorus [21] and fluorine [22]. Doping with different heteroatoms may induce different changes in composition, morphology and structure, presenting distinctive properties [23–25]. Selecting different functionalized molecules to obtain novel GQDs with special functions is the hot spots in the study of graphene quantum dots. d‑Penicillamine, a metabolite of penicillin, has strong complexation with metal ions. It contains functional groups of amino and thiol and can be a good dopant for introduction of N and S atoms into the GQDs. In addition, penicillamine is also very good biocompatibility and friend-environment, it could be used in the human body. So, in this work, with penicillamine as functional molecule, citric acid as carbon source, a novel N,S co-doped GQDs(N,S/GQDs) were synthesized. The obtained N,S/GQDs not only showed higher quantum yield of 56.4% and lower cytotoxicity, but also higher binding capacity for the mercury ions. Based on such a recognition effect, a very sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor for the imaging mercury ions in the living cells was developed.

d‑Penicillamine (DPA) and citric acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Britton Robinson buffer solution (BR buffer, H3PO4-HAc-H3BO3, 0.04 M) was prepared in the laboratory. Standard stock (0.1 M) solution of Hg2+ ions were prepared with Hg(NO3)2. Different concentrations of Hg2+ ions were obtained by diluting standard stock solutions. Fetal bovine serum, penicillin (100 KU/mL) and streptomycin (30 mg/mL) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3‑(4,5‑dimethyl‑2‑thiazolyl)‑2,5‑diphenyl‑2‑H‑tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, china). All materials were used without any further purification. All other reagents are of analytical reagent grade.

TEM images were acquired on transmission electron microscope (FEI-Tecnai G2, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 KV. Atomic force microscopy AFM images were obtained from Dimension Edge (Brucker, Germany) in the ScanAsyst mode under ambient condition. The crystalline phase was investigated by an X'Pert PRO X-ray diffractometer (PANalytical, Holland). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The time-resolved fluorescence decay and the fluorescence quantum yield were performed on an Edinburgh FLS980 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) with a 360 nm LED as the excitation source. All fluorescence spectra were surveyed on an F97 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). UV–vis measurements were carried out on a TU-1810 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) and the spectra were collected from 200 nm to 800 nm. A Bruker Tensor 27 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) was used to collect the IR spectra in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 with KBr pellets. Cells were analyzed using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti-E, Nikon, Japan) equipped with a 100 W mercury lamp (C-SHG1, Nikon). The cell viability was determined by recording the absorbance of formazan at 490 nm on a Multiskan GO microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, Finland).

N,S/GQDs was synthesized as following: Firstly, 0.2 g d‑penicillamine and 0.5 g citric acid were mixed into a flask and heated from room temperature to 160 °C in a heating mantle. Then, keep heating at 160 °C for 9 min, the color of the mixture got orange indicating N,S/GQDs have been generated. After cooling to room temperature, 15.0 mL 1 mg/mL NaOH were added into the reaction solution and centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 20 min. The suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and frozen drying for 48 h. Finally, the obtained solid were dissolved in water as a stock solution (0.1 mg/mL) for future characterization and application.

The sensing system was performed in the 0.1 mol/L pH 3.5 BR buffer. To a 4.0 mL fluorescence cuvette, 50 μL 0.1 mg/mL N,S/GQDs, 20 μL 0.1 M BR buffer (pH = 3.5) buffer solution, some Hg2+ standard solution and appropriate distilled water were added. The final volume was kept 3.0 mL. After reaction for 15 min, the fluorescence intensity was measured at λex/λem = 360/435 nm. The selectivity of Hg2+ sensing was confirmed by adding other competitive mental ions to the solution.

The cellular toxicity experiment was evaluated using the standard MTT assay with Hela cells. 100 μL of cells with a density of 1 × 104 cell/well were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24 h at 37 °C. Thereafter, the culture medium was removed and the cells were treated with DMEM including different concentrations of the N,S/GQDs for 24 h. Subsequently, 10 μL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well. After an incubation of 4 h, the medium was discard and a solvent of DMSO (100 μL) was added to dissolve formazan crystal. Finally, after shaking for 10 min, the absorption intensity of each sample was recorded through a microplate reader.

Hela cells (human cervical cancer cell) were seeded in a DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) supplemented with 100 units/mL penicillin, 100 mg/mL streptomycin and 5% CO2 at 37 °C. For fluorescence imaging, the cells were incubated with 50 μL 0.1 mg/mL of the N,S/GQDs at 37 °C for 2 h. Another group of N,S/GQDs treated cells was then incubated with 40 μM Hg(NO3)2 for another 0.5 h before imaging. Prior to imaging, all the above cells were washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) for three times.

The as-prepared N,S-GQDs was characterized by TEM, AFM, XRD, XPS and IR respectively. The TEM image (Fig. 1A) showed that the obtained N,S/GQDs particles are well dispersed spherical particles with a narrow size distribution in the range of 2.8–3.5 nm and an average diameter of about 3.15 nm. The HRTEM image of N,S/GQDs reveals a high crystallinity with a lattice fringe distance of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (1120) lattice planes of graphene (Fig. 1B) [26]. AFM image of the N,S/GQDs (Fig. 1C) and its height distribution (Fig. 1D) indicates that their heights are no more than 1.8 nm, suggesting that there are one to four layers of graphene oxide-like structures [27]. A wide (002) peak centered at 23.5° of the XRD (Fig. 1E) pattern suggested the graphene-like structure of N,S/GQDs. The XPS (Fig. 2A) spectrum of N,S/GQDs clearly demonstrated four dominant peaks of binding energy at 284.78, 400.42,163.75 and 530.94 eV, which are originated from C1s, N1s, S2p and O1s, respectively. The atomic percentage of C, O, N, S and were found to be 49.2%, 34.33%, 9.92%, 6.56%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2B) can be well deconvoluted into three surface components, corresponding to (CC/CC) at 284.7 ev, CO(N, S) at 285.4 ev, CO at 288.0 ev, respectively. The two peaks of N1s (Fig. 2C) and S2p (Fig. 2D) at 400.0 eV and 400.4 eV and 163.6 eV and 164.5 eV suggested the occurring of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [28] and the presence of CSC units [29]. To further determine the functional groups of the N,S/GQDs, the IR spectrum was also investigated (Fig. 2E). The broad absorption bands of IR at 3420 cm−1 are a characteristic peak of the stretching vibration of OH and NH. The peak at 2539.30 cm−1 is suggested as stretching vibration of SH. The bands around 1712.59 cm−1,1607.99 cm−1, 1393.50 cm−1 can be assigned to asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of COO, which indicate the presence of carbonyl functional groups in the N,S/GQDs [30]. A broad absorption at 1235 cm−1 is related to the vibrational stretching of CN, CO, CS groups [31]. From the above mentioned, we can concluded that the N,S/GQDs with abundant carboxyl, amino and thiol groups have been successfully synthesized.

The N,S/GQDs (Fig. S1A) has two clear absorption bands of 219 nm and 352 nm in UV–vis absorption which originates from the π-π* transition of aromatic CC and the n-π* transition of the CO bone [32,33], which suggesting the polycyclic heterocyclic structure of the graphene layer and the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of N,S/GQDs, respectively. With increasing excitation wavelengths, the emission peak position shifts to longer wavelengths and the intensity decreases rapidly suggesting the excitation-independent character of the emission wavelength of N,S/GQDs (Fig. S1B). This may be due to the presence of different particle size and different emission sites on the surface of the N,S/GQDs. The maximum emission intensity of N,S/GQDs was achieved at 435 nm when they excited at 360 nm. The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared N,S/GQDs was calculated to be 56.4% at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which was much higher than the mostly reported QYS of GQDs [27,29,34]. At the same time, the optical stability of N,S/GQDs also was investigated under different pH (Fig. S2B), ionic strength solution (Fig. S2A) and a long time placement under a natural light irradiation (Fig. S2C). The fluorescence intensity had almost no change whether in the broad range of 3.0–11.0, or under high ionic strengths even up to 1.0 mol/L of NaCl or long time placement of 45 days which proved the excellent photostability of the obtained N,S/GQDs.

As the fluorescence spectra showed (Fig. 3A), the maximum emission peek of N,S/GQDs red-shifted with an remarkable fluorescence quenching with the addition of Hg2+. According to the reported publications [35,36] the wavelength shift suggested the structure change of N,S/GQDs which could be originated from the formation of complex between Hg2+ and the functional groups of N,S/GQDs such as amino-group, thiol and carboxyl. Such a complex formation could also be proved again by UV–vis spectra of N,S/GQDs after the addition of Hg2+ as shown in Fig. 3B. To further determine the above assume, firstly, the comparison of the quenching of Hg2+ for the un-doped GQDs (Fig. 3C) and N,S/GQDs (Fig. 3D) was carried out, it could be seen that Hg2+ has a much stronger quenching effect on the N,S/GQDs than the un-doped GQDs which suggested that the doped N and S played an important role on the recognition of Hg2+. Such an improvement could be ascribed to the coordination interaction between Hg2+ and the functional groups of the obtained N,S/GQDs. To affirm the coordination, second, the FT-IR of N,S/GQDs and the complex N,S/GQDs-Hg2+ were investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3E, when Hg2+ is present in N,S/GQDs solution, the peak position of OH stretching vibration band moves from 3420 to 3431 cm−1, together with the narrowing of the peak. The characteristic IR absorption peak of SH at 2539 cm−1 decreased greatly, which may be attributed to the formation of SHg bonding by the deprotonation of the thiol group. The asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate ion (COO) have moved from 1607 and 1393 to 1635 and 1384 cm−1, respectively suggesting the bonding interaction between Hg2+ and the COO group of N,S/GQDs [37]. Therefore, according to the above changes of the obtained N,S/GQDs before and after adding Hg2+, we could concluded that the coordination between the COO, OH, SH and Hg2+ resulted in the enhancement of quenching efficiency of N,S/GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the Detection System of N,S/GQDs-Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: To get the better analysis behaviors, the pH and consumption of buffers, reaction time of N,S/GQDs-Hg2+ were optimized (Fig. S3A, B and C). According to the quenching rate of fluorescence (F0 − F)/F0 (F0 and F are the fluorescence of N,S/GQDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+), the detection conditions were selected as 20 μL pH 3.5 BR buffer solution and reaction for 15 min. Under the optimum conditions, the fluorescence quenching rate (F0 − F)/F0 was lineage with the concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0.9–30.0 nM (Fig. 4A and B), the regression equation are y = 0.00312C + 0.00489, where C is the concentration of Hg2+. The detection of limit is estimated to be 0.69 nM according to 3σ/k, in which σ is the standard deviation of the 11 detection for the blank solution, and k is the slope of the calibration curve. Compared with other doped graphene dots or carbon quantum dots (Table 1 ), the obtained N,S/GQDs in our work has higher quantum yields and higher sensitivity for the Hg2+ which would improve the practice application of the proposed method further [27,36,38–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N,S/GQDs for Hg2+ Detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the detection specificity of the present detection system, other mental ions was added into the detection system of N,S/GQDs-Hg2+, the concentration of Hg2+ is 100 nM, the fluorescence intensity of N,S/GQDs-Hg2+ with and without the interference were marked as F0 and F, respectively. The ratio of (F0 − F)/F0 was used to evaluate the effect of the interference on the detection system. The value of (F0 − F)/F0 greater than 5% is treated as interference. It could be seen that (Fig. S4A), excepted Fe3+, 100 times (10 μM) of Ag+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ca2+ showed no interference for the determination. The chelating agent of Pyrophosphate was added, it could be seen that (Fig. S4B) 0.33 mM Pyrophosphate had no influence on the fluorescence of N,S/GQDs and N,S/GQDs-Hg2+, but it could eliminated the interference of 0.16 mM Fe3+. So, in the experiment, some appropriate Pyrophosphate was added into the detection system to shield the interference from Fe3+.

To evaluate the practicality of the sensing method, the practical application of this sensing system was investigated using standard addition method for Hg2+ detection in lack water from Zhengzhou University. The water was centrifuged and filtered, then adjusted to pH = 3.5 with BR solution. After 20 μL water sample was added into the reaction system, no obvious fluorescence change was found. Some standard solutions of Hg2+ were added into the water samples and detected (Table 2 ). The recoveries rate for three adding concentrations were ranged from 99.63%–104.73%, the RSD of four times were in the range of 2.1%–4.0% which proved the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method.

As shown in (Fig. 5A), HeLa cells presented viability higher than 85% after incubation with N,S/GQDs for 24 h in the concentration of 0, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL, which indicated the low cell toxicity and good biocompatibility of N,S/GQDs. So, they were applied in the living cell imaging. It could be seen that, relative to the HeLa cells incubated with the DMEM culture medium (Fig. 5B), the HeLa cells incubated with the N,S/GQDs had obvious blue fluorescence (Fig. 5C), which proved the excellent cell permeability of the as-prepared N,S/GQDs. When 40 μM Hg2+ was added into the HeLa cells with blue fluorescence, the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenching (Fig. 5D). This indicated that the N,S/GQDs could be efficiently quenched under the intracellular environment conditions, proving clear evidence of fluorescence quenching-based imaging Hg2+ in living cells.

In this work, a novel N,S/GQDs were synthesized via one-pot pyrolysis using citric acid as carbon source, d‑penicillamine as doping molecule. The obtained N,S/GQDs were high quantum yield of 56.4% and stable against long time placement. Taking advantage of the excellent fluorescence properties of N,S/GQDs, a sensitive and selective detection method for Hg2+ was established. Furthermore, N,S/GQDs had low cell toxicity and good biocompatibility and were successfully applied for the imaging Hg2+ in the living cell.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (U140424), the Key Scientific Research Project of Henan Province (17A150051).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.07.097.
